question: What are the default size limits for file uploads and total request size for the new multipart upload support on the OpenAI-compatible API endpoints?
gold_answer: The default limits are 10 MiB per file (max_file_size) and 50 MiB total per request (max_total_request_size) for multipart uploads on the OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
answer_facts: The default per file upload size limit (max_file_size) for multipart uploads on OpenAI-compatible endpoints is 10 MiB.
The default total request size limit (max_total_request_size) for multipart uploads on OpenAI-compatible endpoints is 50 MiB.

question: What is the name of the new metric added so SRE can track when server-side streaming sessions get finalized due to hitting the time limit?
gold_answer: The new metric is `stream.timebox_finalized` (with labels for route and model).
answer_facts: The new metric added is named stream.timebox_finalized
The metric stream.timebox_finalized includes labels for route and model

gold_answer: The acceptance criteria are: (1) deliver a stable design JSON token spec including the base palette, interactive derivative tokens (e.g., color.interactive.primary.hover), and elevation.kappa.0-6 with explicit shadow parameters; (2) land an implementation-ready tokens PR in the frontend repo, with DataGrid and Table consuming the new tokens behind a feature flag; (3) pass accessibility contrast checks for all interactive states (3:1 where applicable and 4.5:1 for primary actionable text); (4) add per-component visual regression snapshots/tolerances with no regressions beyond approved deltas; (5) update the Figma design kit with tokenized components and guidance notes; and (6) execute a rollout plan from internal canary on analytics dashboards to staged rollout and full migration with a fallback.
answer_facts: Deliver a stable design JSON token spec that includes the base palette, interactive derivative tokens (for example color.interactive.primary.hover), and elevation.kappa.0-6 with explicit shadow parameters
Land an implementation-ready tokens PR in the frontend repo with DataGrid and Table consuming the new tokens behind a feature flag
Pass accessibility contrast checks for all interactive states, meeting 3:1 where applicable and 4.5:1 for primary actionable text
Add per-component visual regression snapshots and tolerances with no regressions beyond approved deltas
Update the Figma design kit with tokenized components and guidance notes
Execute a rollout plan from an internal canary on analytics dashboards to staged rollout and full migration with a fallback

gold_answer: The GCP team said entitlement propagation delays can happen, so the onboarding UX should handle a "pending" state gracefully-avoid scary "not entitled" errors, tell the user the subscription is still syncing and may take a few minutes, and provide a clear retry/refresh experience (optionally showing when it was last checked) plus support instructions if it doesn't resolve.
answer_facts: Entitlement propagation delays can occur after a new subscription.
The onboarding user experience should handle an entitlement pending state gracefully.
The onboarding flow should avoid showing scary not entitled errors during propagation delays.
The onboarding flow should tell the user the subscription is still syncing and may take a few minutes.
The onboarding flow should provide a clear retry or refresh experience, optionally showing when it was last checked.
The onboarding flow should provide support instructions if the entitlement delay does not resolve.

gold_answer: MedThink's preferred failover hierarchy is EU primary → EU warm standby → US emergency failover. They set a target RPO of 15 minutes for active datasets and an RTO target of 30 minutes for critical APIs, and allow shifting to the US only during declared, pre-approved emergency windows capped at 4 hours.
answer_facts: MedThink specified a failover hierarchy of EU primary to EU warm standby to US emergency failover for an EU region outage.
MedThink set an RPO target of 15 minutes for active datasets.
MedThink set an RTO target of 30 minutes for critical APIs.
MedThink allows shifting traffic to the US only during declared, pre-approved emergency windows capped at 4 hours.

gold_answer:
The draft proposes this canonical v1 signal priority order: 
                            (1) authoritative Reachability DOWN → FAILOVER (unless no healthy fallback), 
                            (2) fast/severe 5xx error burn → FAILOVER or PARTIAL_SHIFT depending on blast radius and capacity, 
                            (3) tail latency burn → PARTIAL_SHIFT first, escalating to FAILOVER if corroborated and sustained, 
                            (4) capacity exhaustion/hard gate → HOLD plus load shed (avoid shifting into constrained regions), and 
                            (5) control plane degradation → HOLD to avoid rapid flips/oscillation.
answer_facts: The draft spec proposes a canonical v1 priority order for evaluating failure signals when deciding whether to shift traffic or fail over.
Highest priority is authoritative Reachability DOWN, which triggers FAILOVER unless there is no healthy fallback.
Second priority is fast or severe 5xx error burn, which triggers FAILOVER or PARTIAL_SHIFT depending on blast radius and capacity.
Third priority is tail latency burn, which triggers PARTIAL_SHIFT first and escalates to FAILOVER if corroborated and sustained.
Fourth priority is capacity exhaustion or a hard gate, which triggers HOLD plus load shed to avoid shifting traffic into constrained regions.
Fifth priority is control plane degradation, which triggers HOLD to avoid rapid flips or oscillation.
